hoses: Hydrant supply hose from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
Portable monitor supply line A: 200 ft of 2½-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to portable monitor
Portable monitor supply line B: 200 ft of 2½-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to portable monitor
nozzles: Deck gun master stream, 500 GPM @ 80 PSI
Portable monitor master stream, 500 GPM @ 80 PSI
appliances: Portable monitor supplied by two hoses from Engine 181
sceneElements.elevation: Level industrial parking lot, 0 PSI elevation correction

problems.title: Deck Gun Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: The deck gun is mounted directly on Engine 181 and uses 80 PSI nozzle pressure. The stream is operating 30 ft above the pump; use elevation pressure = height × 0.5 PSI/ft. There is no discharge hose friction loss or appliance loss. What pump discharge pressure is required for the deck gun?
problems.instructorExplanation: The deck gun has no hose stretch, but the stated elevation still must be added to nozzle pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Do not enter nozzle pressure alone when the problem gives an elevation add-on.

problems.title: Portable Monitor Flow Per Line
problems.question: The portable monitor flows 500 GPM total and is supplied by two equal hose lines from Engine 181. The two lines split the monitor flow; they do not each flow the full monitor total. What flow should be used for each monitor supply line?
formulaBreakdown: Flow per line = total monitor flow / number of supply lines.
problems.instructorExplanation: The monitor flow is split evenly between the two supply lines. Each line carries 250 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use the full 500 GPM in each line. The two monitor supply lines share the total flow.

problems.title: Portable Monitor Supply Line Friction Loss
problems.question: Each portable monitor supply line is 200 ft of 2½-inch hose. The portable monitor flows 500 GPM total through two equal lines, so calculate each line at one-half of the total flow. Using FL = C × (GPM/100)² × (length/100), with C = 2, what is the friction loss in each supply line?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Each monitor supply line carries 250 GPM. At 250 GPM through 200 ft of 2½-inch hose, the friction loss is 25 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate friction loss using 500 GPM in one line. The portable monitor flow is split between two equal supply lines.

problems.title: Portable Monitor Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Engine 181 is supplying a portable monitor through two equal 200 ft 2½-inch supply lines. The monitor flows 500 GPM total; split the flow equally between the two lines for the hose friction-loss calculation. Nozzle pressure is 80 PSI, elevation is 0 PSI, and portable monitor appliance loss is 25 PSI. What pump discharge pressure is required for the portable monitor?
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
Friction loss per supply line = 25 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: For a portable monitor supplied by two equal lines, calculate one supply path. Add friction loss, nozzle pressure, elevation, and appliance loss for a pump discharge pressure of 130 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add the friction loss of both monitor supply lines together. Equal parallel lines split the flow, and one supply path governs the pressure.

problems.title: Governing Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Engine 181 is supplying both a deck gun and a portable monitor. The deck gun is mounted directly on the engine, uses 80 PSI nozzle pressure, and is operating 30 ft above the pump. The portable monitor flows 500 GPM total through two equal 200 ft 2½-inch supply lines. Use C = 2, split the monitor flow equally between the two lines, use 80 PSI monitor nozzle pressure, add 25 PSI monitor appliance loss, and use no elevation for the monitor. Which pump discharge pressure should govern the operation?
formulaBreakdown: The higher pressure path governs: 130 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: Calculate the portable monitor path using the split flow per line, then compare it to the deck gun path. Use the higher required pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add the deck gun pressure to the monitor pressure. Separate devices do not stack pressures; the highest pressure path governs.
